Summary: This study developed and evaluated a 3D deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) for automated liver segmentation, volumetry, and radiomic feature extraction on contrast-enhanced portal venous phase MRI. The DCNN demonstrated robust and generalizable segmentation performance on internal, external, and public testing data, with excellent agreement in liver volumetry and radiomic feature extraction compared to manual segmentation.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to any specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study clearly defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, aiming for a representative study population. The inclusion criteria are patients >18 years old with T1-weighted portal venous MR images available for processing. Exclusion criteria included patients <18 years, with non-diagnostic MRI, and no portal venous phase MRI.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is manual segmentation performed under the supervision of two board-certified abdominal radiologists with 9 and 10 years of experience, respectively, which is a reasonable reference standard.

Item4.reason: The study uses data from Yale School of Medicine and Beaujon Hospital in Paris, indicating a multi-center approach.

Item5.reason: The imaging data source is contrast-enhanced portal venous phase MRI, which is a clinically relevant and standardized imaging technique for liver assessment, as suggested by the LIRADS comity.

Item6.reason: The study reports the imaging protocol, including the T1-weighted 3D gradient echo VIBE sequence with fat saturation, contrast administration timing, and scanner field strengths. More information about imaging parameters can be found in Supplemental Table 1.

Item7.reason: The study uses portal venous phase MRI to assess liver segmentation, volumetry, and radiomic features in HCC patients. The time interval between imaging and diagnosis is appropriate to validate the presence or absence of target conditions of the radiomics analysis at the moment of the diagnostic imaging exams.

Condition1.reason: The study includes liver segmentation using a DCNN, with manual segmentation used as ground truth.

Condition2.reason: The study uses a DCNN to automatically segment the liver on 3D T1-weighted portal venous MR images using manual liver segmentations as ground truth, indicating fully automated segmentation.

Item8.reason: The study mentions that all livers were manually segmented under the supervision of two board-certified abdominal radiologists (S.A. and S.H. with 9 and 10 years of experience, respectively) using the software 3D Slicer (v4.11).

Item9.reason: The study quantifies segmentation accuracy using DSC, MHD, and MAD metrics between manual and automated liver segmentations and presents the results.

Item10.reason: The study does not explicitly state whether the test set segmentation masks were produced by a single reader or automated tool, but it is implied that the automated tool produced the final segmentation in the test set.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts radiomic features, which are hand-crafted features.

Item11.reason: The study mentions that input images were resized to have 2x2x2 mm voxel spacings and normalized such that the 25th and 75th image intensity percentiles were scaled to –0.5 and +0.5, respectively.

Item12.reason: The study used PyRadiomics (v3.0) for feature extraction, which is a standardized software.

Item13.reason: The study describes the radiomic features extracted (first-order statistics, GLCM, GLDM, GLRLM, GLSZM, NGTDM, and shape features) and provides the PyRadiomics settings used for feature extraction in Supplement 3.

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of radiomic features extracted from the images.

Condition5.reason: The study uses an end-to-end deep learning approach for liver segmentation.

Item14.reason: The study does not mention the removal of non-robust features.

Item15.reason: The study does not mention the removal of redundant features.

Item16.reason: The study does not discuss the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size.

Item17.reason: The study does not include a robustness assessment of the end-to-end deep learning pipeline.

Item18.reason: The study mentions that the institutional dataset was randomly split into training, validation, and internal testing subsets containing 70/15/15% of the data, respectively.

Item19.reason: The study acknowledges the presence of image artifacts and variations in image quality due to different scanners and contrast agents, and reports that no substantial segmentation performance differences were noted across different clinical findings or in cases with image artifacts or reduced image quality, indicating robust generalizability.

Item20.reason: The study uses appropriate performance evaluation metrics such as DSC, MHD, MAD, and ICC for segmentation and volumetry tasks.

Item21.reason: The study includes uncertainty measures such as standard deviation (SD) and 95% confidence intervals (CI) for ICC values.

Item22.reason: The study does not include a calibration assessment of the final model.

Item23.reason: The study relies only on a single-contrast phase, which is T1-weighted portal venous phase contrast-enhanced MRI.

Item24.reason: The study does not include a comparison with a non-radiomic approach or proof of added clinical value.

Item25.reason: The study does not include a comparison with simple or classical statistical models.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an internal test set.

Item27.reason: The model is tested on an external test set from a collaborating institution and a public dataset.
